sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 4-year-old twins' mother, injured her right knee while skiing in Vermont last weekend.
She caught her ski and fell, causing her knee to pop and resulting in persistent pain since then.
She was unable to bear weight and had to be taken down by the ski patrol.
The pain is located on the outside of the knee, and she feels like the knee will buckle when bearing weight.
She was initially able to bend the knee but has lost this ability since the injury.
Straightening the knee is also painful, but she can straighten it somewhat.
She has no numbness or tingling in her toes and can feel them.
She is generally healthy, with no high blood pressure, diabetes, or other chronic conditions.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: I prescribed ibuprofen 600-800 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has edema and an effusion over the right knee, with pain to palpation of the right lateral knee.
There is decreased flexion and extension of the right knee, and a positive Lachman sign.
The dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse are palpable, and there is no leg edema in the ankle.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: I suspect a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in the right knee.
Problem list: ACL tear; suspected; confirmed by physical exam; unknown status of other knee structures.
Plan:
I will order a knee MRI to confirm the suspected ACL tear and rule out other potential injuries.
I will also put the patient in a brace to provide support to the knee.
The patient will continue to take ibuprofen 600-800 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
Follow-up: The patient will be scheduled for an MRI in the next couple of days, and we will discuss the results and further management at that time.
